title: practical english usage - Part 62
content:
Have (got) to is also possible in this sense.

There’s the doorbell. It must  be George.

Emily must  have a problem - she keeps crying.

'I’m  in love.’ ’ You must  be very happy.’ If A is bigger than B, and B is bigger than C, then A must  be bigger than C.

believing it, or it is the only possible explanation for what is happening.

certain or highly probable: it is normal or logical, there are excellent reasons for Must can be used to express the deduction or conclusion that something is 1  statements:       Emily     must      have      a  problem.

certain):    m  ust,    ca  n't,  e tc 69 deduction        (deciding      that    something        is contracted affirmative forms ('ll, 'd). For more about contractions, ►    337.

oughtn’t, mightn't (BrE) and mustn ’t (mostly BrE).  Will and would also have Contracted negatives are won’t, wouldn’t, shan't (BrE), shouldn’t, can’t, couldn’t, Section 7 Modal    Auxiliary      Verbs grammar •  69  deduction (deciding that something is certain): must, can’t, etc not Next week's exam should/ought to be easy, (     Next week's exam not - It will be fine tomorrow, (     It must be fine tomorrow.) suggest that things are probable (►  70).

expect to happen.  Will is used to express certainty (►   38); should or ought to Must is not often used for predictions about the future - to say what people 9 must:     not   used    for   predictions Note that the contracted form mustn ’t is rare in AmE.

(most British speakers.) She walked past without saying 'Hello'. She can't have seen you.

(AmE; some British speakers.) She walked past without saying 'Hello'. She must  not have seen you.

must not for the 'seems pretty certain’  meaning). Compare: In British English, can't is normal for both meanings (though some people use That restaurant must  not be any good - it's always empty.

- The restaurant can't be open - the door’s locked.

impossible that she’ s home, but it seems pretty certain that she isn't.) She’s not answering the doorbell. She must  not be at home. (It's not logically impossible that he’ s home.) - He only left the office five minutes ago. He can't be home yet. (It's logically case. Compare: impossible, but when there is strong evidence for believing that it is not the In American English, must not is often used when something is not logically 8  a  British-American         difference:       can't     and   must      not Anna should be at home by now. (= I think she’ s very probably at home.) Anna must  be at home by now. (= I’ m  certain she’ s at home.) Should can be used as a weaker form of must (►   70). Compare: 7 must      and   should I felt there must  be something wrong.

Must can be used in past indirect speech as if it were a past tense.

6 indirect      speech For more about will as a future auxiliary, ►    38.

We can't go and see them now - they'll have gone to bed.

Dear Sir, You will recently have received a form.. .

Will have + past participle refers to the past.

Tomorrow will be cloudy, with some rain.

'There’s somebody coming up the stairs.’ 'That'll be Emily.’ Don't phone them now - they'll be having dinner.

As I'm sure you will understand, we cannot wait any longer for our order.

Will can express certainty or confidence about present or future situations.

grammar *71   chances: may, might and could 'I think it's going to rain.' 'You may well be right - the sky's really black.' May well, might well and could well suggest stronger possibilities.

They've agreed, but they could could change their minds.

'Where's Emma? ' 'I don't know. She might be out running I suppose.' Daniel might phone. If he does, ask him  to ring later.

'I think Labour are going to win.* 'You may be right.* We may go climbing in the Alps next summer.

something will happen, or is happening.

We   often use may, might and could to talk about the chance (possibility) that 1  You     may     be   right;     W e  may     go    climbing.

71 chances:     may,     might       and   could For more about structures with ought, ►    76.3.

For be supposed to to express obligation, ►    78.
